steps.title: Your Body in Korean
steps.desc:
Before you can say what hurts, you need the vocabulary.

13 body parts, most with Sino-Korean compounds that unlock dozens of related words.
goals: See Sino-Korean compounds in body/health vocabulary
Prepare for 아프다 (to hurt) in Lesson 2
Build the vocabulary base for Korean healthcare

steps.note:
머리: head (also: hair).

머리가 아파요: My head hurts.
머리카락: hair strands (머리 + 카락).

SINO-KOREAN compound:
두통 = 두 (頭: head) + 통 (痛: pain) = headache.
두 appears in: 두뇌 (brain: head + brain).

The face:
눈: eye(s)
코: nose
입: mouth
귀: ear(s)

All pure Korean words.
눈이 아파요: My eye hurts.
코가 아파요: My nose hurts.

⚠️ 눈 = eye AND snow.
Context always makes it clear:
눈이 와요 (snow comes) vs 눈이 커요 (eyes are big).

목: neck AND throat.

목이 아파요: my throat hurts.
목소리: voice (목 + 소리: throat + sound).
목걸이: necklace (목 + 걸이: neck + hanging thing).

감기에 걸리면 목이 아파요.
= If you catch a cold, your throat hurts.

배: stomach/belly.

배가 아파요: my stomach hurts.
배가 고파요: I'm hungry (stomach is empty).
배가 불러요: I'm full (stomach is swollen).

⚠️ 배 = stomach AND pear AND boat AND double.
Context always clarifies.

손: hand. 발: foot.

Compounds with 손:
손가락: finger (손 + 가락: hand + stick)
손톱: fingernail (손 + 톱)

Compounds with 발:
발가락: toe (발 + 가락: foot + stick)
발목: ankle (발 + 목: foot + neck)

팔: arm. 다리: leg.

팔이 아파요: my arm hurts.
다리가 아파요: my leg hurts.

Compounds:
팔꿈치: elbow (팔 + 꿈치)
무릎: knee
어깨: shoulder

어깨: shoulder.
등: back (upper/middle).
허리: waist/lower back.

허리가 아파요 is one of the most common complaints in Korea.

허리 디스크: herniated disc.
Koreans sit on floors — 허리 problems are cultural.

steps.title: 🦴 Body Parts Map
steps.text:
HEAD: 머리 (head), 눈 (eye), 코 (nose), 입 (mouth), 귀 (ear), 목 (throat/neck)

TORSO: 어깨 (shoulder), 등 (back), 가슴 (chest), 배 (stomach), 허리 (lower back)

LIMBS: 팔 (arm), 손 (hand), 손가락 (finger), 다리 (leg), 발 (foot), 발가락 (toe)

JOINTS: 팔꿈치 (elbow), 무릎 (knee), 발목 (ankle)

All use 이/가 아파요 to say 'X hurts':
머리가 아파요 = My head hurts.
배가 아파요 = My stomach hurts.

이/가 (not 은/는) because the pain is NEW information.

steps.hint: 머리 = head / hair. 머리카락 = hair strands specifically.
통 (痛) = pain. 두통 = head + pain = headache.
소리 = sound. 목소리 = throat + sound = voice.
Pain is new information → 이/가 (subject marker), not 은/는 (topic).
가락 = stick/rod. 손가락 = hand-stick = finger. 발가락 = foot-stick = toe.

steps.s: ___가 아파요. (My throat hurts.)
steps.hint: 목 = throat/neck

steps.s: ___가 아파요. (My lower back hurts.)
steps.hint: 허리 = waist/lower back

steps.s: {1}가 아프고 {2}도 아파요. 감기인 것 같아요.
steps.hint: My throat hurts and my head hurts too. I think it's a cold.
눈 = eye AND snow. Context makes it clear.

steps.title: Saying What Hurts
steps.desc:
아프다 = to hurt / to be sick.

This verb pairs with 이/가 — not 은/는.
Why? Because when something hurts, it's NEW information.

This lesson reinforces the 이/가 vs 은/는 distinction from Unit 5 in a real context.
goals: Understand why 이/가 (not 은/는) for pain

steps.note:
아프다: to hurt, to be sick.

아프다 → 아파요 (bright ㅏ → 아요).

⚠️ BUT: 아프다 is a ㅡ-stem verb.
아프 + 아요 → ㅡ drops → 아파요.
Same ㅡ-drop pattern as 쓰다 → 써요.

어디가 아파요? = Where hurts? = What's wrong?

Pattern: [body part] + 이/가 + 아파요.

이/가 — NOT 은/는.
Why? Because the pain is the NEW information.

Doctor: 어디가 아파요? (Where hurts?)
You: 머리가 아파요. (My HEAD hurts — new info).

If you said 머리는 아파요, it would imply:
'As for my head, it hurts (but other things are fine)' — weird in this context.

감기: cold (illness).
걸리다: to be caught/to catch (illness).

감기에 걸렸어요: caught a cold.

Symptoms of 감기:
목이 아파요 (throat hurts)
기침이 나요 (coughing: 기침 + 나다)
콧물이 나요 (runny nose: 콧물 + 나다)
열이 나요 (have a fever: 열 + 나다)

열 (熱): heat/fever.
나다: to come out / to arise.

열이 나요 = fever arises = I have a fever.

Same 나다 pattern:
기침이 나요: cough comes out = I'm coughing.
콧물이 나요: nose-water comes out = runny nose.
땀이 나요: sweat comes out = I'm sweating.

steps.exampleEn: I'm coughing and have a runny nose too.
steps.note:
기침: cough.
콧물: runny nose (코 + ㅅ + 물: nose + water).

기침이 나요: I'm coughing.
콧물이 나요: I have a runny nose.

코 → 콧물: the ㅅ is added for pronunciation.
Similar: 깻잎 (perilla leaf), 나뭇잎 (tree leaf).

약 (藥): medicine.

약을 먹다: to take medicine.
⚠️ Korean says '먹다' (eat) medicine, not '마시다' (drink).

약국: pharmacy (약 + 국: medicine + bureau).
약사: pharmacist (약 + 사: medicine + person/expert).

steps.title: 🏥 At the Doctor — Key Phrases
steps.text:
어디가 아파요?
= Where does it hurt?

언제부터 아팠어요?
= Since when have you been in pain?

증상이 뭐예요?
= What are the symptoms?

알레르기가 있어요?
= Do you have any allergies?

약을 드세요.
= Please take this medicine. (드시다 = honorific of 먹다)

푹 쉬세요.
= Please rest well. (푹 = deeply/thoroughly)

steps.hint: Pain = new info → 이/가, not 은/는.
감기 = cold. 걸리다 = to catch. 걸렸어요 = caught (past).
Korean says 약을 먹다 (eat medicine), not drink.
나다 = to arise/come out. 열이 나다 = fever arises.

steps.q: 콧물 = 코 + ㅅ + 물. What is 물?
steps.hint: 콧물 = nose + water = runny nose.

steps.s: 감기에 ___. 목이 아파요. (I caught a cold. My throat hurts.)
steps.hint: 걸리다 → past: 걸렸어요 (ㅣ+었 = 렸).

steps.s: ___이 나요. 체온이 38도예요. (I have a fever. Temperature is 38°.)
steps.hint: 열 = fever/heat. 열이 나다 = to have a fever.

steps.s: ___을 먹었어요. 이제 좀 나아요. (I took medicine. I feel a bit better now.)
steps.hint: 약 = medicine. 약을 먹다 = take medicine.

steps.s: 감기에 {1}. {2}가 아프고 {3}이 나요. {4}을 먹었어요.
steps.hint: I caught a cold. My throat hurts and I have a fever. I took medicine.
국 (局): bureau/office. 약국 = medicine bureau = pharmacy.

steps.title: If... Then... — The Conditional
steps.desc:
New grammar: -(으)면.
This is the Korean 'if/when'.

아프면 쉬세요. = If you're sick, rest.
시간이 있으면 같이 가요. = If you have time, let's go together.

One of the most useful grammar points in the language.
goals: Apply to consonant and vowel stems
Use in health and daily life contexts
Combine with verbs, adjectives, and 이다

steps.note:
-(으)면: if / when.

Attaches to STEMS:
Vowel stem → -면: 가다 → 가면 (if you go)
Consonant stem → -으면: 먹다 → 먹으면 (if you eat)
ㄹ stem → -면 (ㄹ stays): 살다 → 살면 (if you live)

아프다 → 아프면 (if sick)

The Train metaphor:
-(으)면 is a CONDITIONAL car.
Stem + [conditional car] + ... main clause verb.

steps.exampleEn: If I go to Korea, I'll eat samgyeopsal.
steps.note:
가다: vowel stem 가.
가 + 면 = 가면.

Vowel stems just add -면 directly.

하다 → 하면 (if you do)
오다 → 오면 (if you come)
보다 → 보면 (if you see/watch)

steps.exampleEn: If you eat a lot, you'll be full.
steps.note:
먹다: consonant stem 먹.
먹 + 으면 = 먹으면.

Consonant stems add -으면.

있다 → 있으면 (if there is / if you have)
없다 → 없으면 (if there isn't)
읽다 → 읽으면 (if you read)

steps.exampleEn: If you're sick, go to the hospital.
steps.note:
아프다: ㅡ stem.
아프 + 면 = 아프면.

ㅡ does NOT drop before -면.
(ㅡ only drops before VOWEL endings like -아/어요.)

크다 → 크면 (if it's big)
바쁘다 → 바쁘면 (if you're busy)
예쁘다 → 예쁘면 (if it's pretty)

steps.exampleEn: If you live in Seoul, you ride the subway a lot.
steps.note:
살다: ㄹ stem.
살 + 면 = 살면. (ㄹ stays before -면.)

ㄹ stems keep ㄹ before 면:
알다 → 알면 (if you know)
만들다 → 만들면 (if you make)

steps.title: 📋 -(으)면 Formation Chart
steps.text:
VOWEL STEM → -면:
가다 → 가면
오다 → 오면
하다 → 하면
보다 → 보면
마시다 → 마시면

CONSONANT STEM → -으면:
먹다 → 먹으면
있다 → 있으면
없다 → 없으면
읽다 → 읽으면

ㅡ STEM → -면 (ㅡ stays):
아프다 → 아프면
크다 → 크면
바쁘다 → 바쁘면

ㄹ STEM → -면 (ㄹ stays):
살다 → 살면
알다 → 알면

IRREGULARS with -(으)면:
듣다 → 들으면 (ㄷ→ㄹ)
덥다 → 더우면 (ㅂ→우)
춥다 → 추우면 (ㅂ→우)

steps.exampleEn: If it's hot, turn on the AC.
steps.note:
덥다: ㅂ-irregular.
덥 → 더우 (ㅂ→우) + 면 = 더우면.

Irregulars change BEFORE -(으)면:
ㄷ-irregular: 듣다 → 들으면
ㅂ-irregular: 덥다 → 더우면, 춥다 → 추우면

The irregular change happens whenever the stem meets a vowel — -(으) counts.

steps.hint: Consonant stem → -으면. 먹 + 으면.
Vowel stem → -면. 가 + 면.
ㅡ stem → -면 (ㅡ stays before consonant). 아프 + 면.
ㅂ-irregular: 덥→더우 + 면 = 더우면.

opts: I have time so let's go
If you have time, let's go together
I'll go when I have time
steps.ans: If you have time, let's go together
steps.hint: 있으면 = if there is / if you have. 같이 = together.

steps.q: 비가 오면 우산을 가져가세요. 오면 means:
steps.hint: -(으)면 = if/when. 비가 오면 = if rain comes = if it rains.

steps.s: ___면 쉬세요. (If you're sick, rest.)
steps.hint: 아프다: ㅡ stem + 면 = 아프면. Drop the 다.

steps.s: 시간이 ___면 영화를 봐요. (If I have time, I watch movies.)
steps.hint: 있다: consonant stem → 있으면.

steps.s: 한국에 ___면 삼겹살을 먹을 거예요. (If I go to Korea, I'll eat samgyeopsal.)
steps.hint: 가다: vowel stem → 가면.

steps.s: {1} 병원에 가세요. {2} 약을 드세요.
steps.hint: If you're sick, go to the hospital. And take medicine.
ㄷ→ㄹ: 듣→들 + 으면 = 들으면.

steps.title: Can / Can't — Ability and Possibility
steps.desc:
New grammar: -(으)ㄹ 수 있다 / 없다.

This means 'can / can't' — but it's different from 못.

못 해요 = I can't do it (right now, situation prevents me).
할 수 있어요 = I have the ability to do it.

Both mean 'can't' in English, but Korean distinguishes them.
goals: Use in health and daily life contexts

steps.note:
-(으)ㄹ 수 있다: can / able to.

Formation:
Vowel stem + ㄹ 수 있다: 가다 → 갈 수 있다
Consonant stem + 을 수 있다: 먹다 → 먹을 수 있다
하다 → 할 수 있다

수 (數): means 'way/method/ability'.
있다: exists.

갈 수 있다 = 'the way to go exists' = can go.

-(으)ㄹ 수 없다: cannot.

같은 pattern, but 없다 instead of 있다:
갈 수 없어요: can't go.
먹을 수 없어요: can't eat.
할 수 없어요: can't do.

수가 없다 = 'the way doesn't exist' = cannot.

steps.title: 💪 못 vs -(으)ㄹ 수 없다
steps.text:
Both translate to 'can't' in English.
But Korean hears them differently:

못 가요: I can't go (situation: I'm busy, it's raining...)
갈 수 없어요: I cannot go (ability/possibility: no bus, road closed...)

못 먹어요: I can't eat (right now: not hungry, sick...)
먹을 수 없어요: I cannot eat (ability: allergic, forbidden...)

In practice, 못 is more casual/spoken.
-(으)ㄹ 수 없다 is more formal/precise.

For abilities/skills:
한국어를 할 수 있어요. ✓ (I CAN speak Korean)
한국어를 못 해요. ✓ (I CAN'T speak Korean)
Both work here — overlapping meaning.

steps.note:
수영하다: to swim.
수영 (水泳): 수 (water) + 영 (swim).

수영하다 → 수영할 수 있어요.

수영할 수 있어요? = Can you swim?
네, 수영할 수 있어요. = Yes, I can swim.
아니요, 수영할 수 없어요. = No, I can't swim.

운전하다: to drive.
운전 (運轉): 운 (carry/move) + 전 (turn/rotate).

운전할 수 있어요? = Can you drive?
운전면허: driver's license (운전 + 면허: driving + license).

가져다 주다: to bring (for someone).
가져다: carry-and-deliver.
주다: to give.

⚡ JRTF: -아/어 주다 = 'do for someone.'
This is a very important pattern.
Full explanation in Unit 15.
For now: 가져다 줘요 = please bring (for me).

steps.hint: Consonant stem + 을 수 있다. 먹 + 을 수 있어요.
Vowel stem + ㄹ 수 있다. 가 + ㄹ = 갈 수 있어요.

steps.q: 못 해요 vs 할 수 없어요:
steps.hint: Both = 'can't do.' 못 is casual/spoken, -(으)ㄹ 수 없다 is more formal.
-(으)ㄹ 수 있다 = can. 수영할 수 있어요 = can you swim?

steps.q: 한국어를 할 수 있어요. 수 means:
steps.hint: 수 (數) here = way/method. 할 수 있다 = 'the way to do exists.'
전 (轉) = turn/rotate. 운전 = carry + turn = driving.

steps.s: 한국어를 ___ 수 있어요. (I can speak Korean.)
steps.hint: 하다 → 할 수 있다. 하 + ㄹ = 할.

steps.s: 오늘 ___ 수 없어요. (I can't go today.)
steps.hint: 가다 → 갈 수 없다. 가 + ㄹ = 갈.

steps.s: 매운 음식을 ___ 수 있어요? (Can you eat spicy food?)
steps.hint: 먹다 → 먹을 수 있다. 먹 + 을 = 먹을.

steps.s: 한국어를 {1} 수 있어요. 하지만 일본어를 {2} 수 없어요.
steps.hint: I can speak Korean. But I can't speak Japanese.
가다 → 갈 수 없다. Sick → can't go to school.

steps.title: Don't Do That! — Negative Commands
steps.desc:
New grammar: -지 마세요.
This is the polite way to say 'don't do X.'

걱정하지 마세요. = Don't worry.
먹지 마세요. = Don't eat (that).

Simple, high-frequency, essential.
goals: Understand politeness levels of negative commands

steps.note:
-지 마세요: please don't.

Formation: stem + 지 마세요.

가다 → 가지 마세요 (please don't go)
먹다 → 먹지 마세요 (please don't eat)
하다 → 하지 마세요 (please don't do)
걱정하다 → 걱정하지 마세요 (please don't worry)

마세요 comes from 말다 (to stop/cease) + 세요 (polite request).

This is the POLITE form.
반말: -지 마 (don't! — casual).

걱정하다: to worry.
걱정 (憂慮): worry/concern.

걱정하지 마세요 is one of the most common phrases in Korean.

괜찮아요: it's okay/fine.
괜찮아요, 걱정하지 마세요.
= It's fine, don't worry.

steps.exampleEn: If you're sick, don't overdo it.
steps.note:
무리하다: to overdo/overexert.
무리 (無理): unreasonable/excessive.
무 (無: without) + 리 (理: reason).

무리하지 마세요: don't push yourself too hard.
Very common health advice in Korean.

늦다: to be late.
늦 + 지 마세요 = 늦지 마세요.

약속에 늦지 마세요: Don't be late for the appointment.
약속 (約束): promise/appointment.

steps.title: 📋 -지 마세요 Common Phrases
steps.text:
Health context:
무리하지 마세요 (don't overdo it)
걱정하지 마세요 (don't worry)
찬 것 먹지 마세요 (don't eat cold things)
술 마시지 마세요 (don't drink alcohol)

Daily life:
늦지 마세요 (don't be late)
잊지 마세요 (don't forget)
만지지 마세요 (don't touch)
가지 마세요 (don't go)

Signs:
들어가지 마세요 (don't enter)
주차하지 마세요 (don't park)
사진 찍지 마세요 (don't take photos)

steps.hint: Stem + 지 마세요. 먹 + 지 마세요.
-지 마세요 = please don't. 걱정하다 = to worry.
반말 form of -지 마세요 is -지 마.
무 (無) = without/not. 무리 = without reason = unreasonable/excessive.

steps.q: 아프면 ___. (If you're sick, don't overdo it.)
steps.hint: -지 마세요 = please don't. Advice: don't overdo it.

steps.s: 내일 ___지 마세요! (Don't be late tomorrow!)
steps.hint: 늦다 → stem 늦 + 지 마세요.

steps.s: 찬 것 ___지 마세요. (Don't eat cold things.)
steps.hint: 먹다 → stem 먹 + 지 마세요.

steps.s: ___하지 마세요. 괜찮아요. (Don't worry. It's okay.)
steps.hint: 걱정하다 = to worry. 걱정하지 마세요.

steps.s: 감기에 걸렸어요. 의사가 말했어요: "{1} 마시지 마세요. {2} 쉬세요."
steps.hint: I caught a cold. The doctor said: 'Don't drink alcohol. Rest well.'
사진 찍다 = take photos. -지 마세요 = please don't.
-(으)면 on first clause, -지 마세요 on second: if sick, don't overdo.

steps.en: may / it's okay to / can I...?
steps.note:
-아/어도 되다: it's okay to / may I?

Formation: stem + 아/어도 되다.
앉다 → 앉아도 돼요? (May I sit?)
가다 → 가도 돼요? (May I go?)
먹다 → 먹어도 돼요? (May I eat?)

돼요 = 되어요 (contracted).

Answer yes: 네, -아/어도 돼요. (Yes, you may.)
Answer no: 아니요, -면 안 돼요. (No, you may not.)

steps.en: may not / not allowed / must not
steps.exampleEn: You must not take photos here.
steps.note:
-(으)면 안 되다: not allowed / must not.

여기에서 담배 피우면 안 돼요.
= You must not smoke here.

이거 먹으면 안 돼요.
= You must not eat this.

-지 마세요 (don't do) = polite request.
-(으)면 안 돼요 = it's not allowed / forbidden.

-지 마세요: softer (please don't).
-(으)면 안 돼요: stronger (you must not).

steps.hint: -아/어도 되다 = may / it's okay to.
-(으)면 안 되다 = not allowed.

steps.title: Hospital and Pharmacy
steps.desc:
Korean healthcare vocabulary with cultural context.

Korea has universal healthcare — 건강보험.
Knowing these words is practical survival Korean.
goals: Understand 의원 vs 병원 vs 대학병원 distinction

steps.exampleEn: I need to go to the hospital.
steps.note:
병원 (病院): 병 (illness) + 원 (院: institution).

⚠️ In Korean, 병원 is used loosely.
It can mean a small clinic OR a large hospital.

The precise distinction:
의원 (醫院): small clinic (1 doctor)
병원 (病院): medium hospital (beds, departments)
대학병원: university hospital (large, specialists)
응급실: emergency room

의사 (醫師): doctor.
의 (醫: medicine/heal) + 사 (師: master/expert).

간호사 (看護師): nurse.
간호 (看護: care/nursing) + 사 (師: expert).

In Korean, doctors are addressed as:
의사 선생님 (Doctor, literally: doctor + teacher + honorific)
or just 선생님 in conversation.

steps.exampleEn: Take the prescription to the pharmacy.
steps.note:
처방전 (處方箋): prescription.
처방 (treatment method) + 전 (slip/paper).

약국 (藥局): pharmacy.
약 (medicine) + 국 (bureau).

Korean system:
1. 의원/병원에서 진료 (see doctor)
2. 처방전 받기 (get prescription)
3. 약국에서 약 받기 (pick up medicine at pharmacy)

Doctors and pharmacies are SEPARATE in Korea.

증상 (症狀): symptoms.
증 (症: illness/condition) + 상 (狀: state/form).

Common 증상:
두통 (headache): 두 (head) + 통 (pain)
복통 (stomachache): 복 (belly) + 통 (pain)
열 (fever)
기침 (cough)
구토 (vomiting): 구 (mouth) + 토 (spit out)

주사 (注射): injection.
주 (注: pour/inject) + 사 (射: shoot).

주사를 맞다: to get a shot.
맞다: to be hit / to receive.

주사를 맞았어요: got a shot (past).

In Korea, injections are MUCH more common than in the West.
Doctors give injections for colds, fatigue, vitamins.

steps.title: 🏥 Korean Healthcare Culture
steps.text:
Korea has universal healthcare: 건강보험 (health insurance).
건강 (健康): health. 보험 (保險): insurance.

Key facts:
• Everyone is covered by 건강보험
• Hospital visits are affordable (~₩5,000–15,000 for clinic)
• Pharmacies fill prescriptions — cheap (~₩3,000–10,000)
• Injections are common and popular
• 한의원 (Korean traditional medicine clinic) uses 한약 (herbal medicine)

의원 vs 병원 vs 대학병원:
감기? → 의원 (small clinic)
수술? → 병원 (hospital)
심각한 병? → 대학병원 (university hospital)
응급? → 응급실 (ER)

Koreans go to the doctor OFTEN.
A cold? Doctor visit. Tired? Doctor visit.
It's affordable and normalized.

deepDive.title: 한의원 — Traditional Korean Medicine
deepDive.text:
한의원: Korean traditional medicine clinic.
한의사: traditional medicine doctor.
한약: herbal medicine.

한 (韓): Korean. 의 (醫): medicine.

Korean traditional medicine uses:
침 (acupuncture)
뜸 (moxibustion)
한약 (herbal medicine)
부항 (cupping)

Many Koreans use BOTH:
양의원 (Western clinic) for acute issues
한의원 (traditional clinic) for chronic/preventive care

Both are covered by 건강보험.

steps.hint: 병 (病) = illness/disease. 병원 = illness + institution.
Prescription → pharmacy. In Korea, doctors and pharmacies are separate.
맞다 = to receive/be hit. 주사를 맞다 = receive an injection.

steps.q: 감기에 걸렸어요. Which do you visit first?
steps.hint: A cold = small clinic (의원). Save 병원/대학병원 for serious issues.
한의원 = Korean (한) + medicine (의) + clinic (원).

steps.s: ___을 가지고 약국에 가세요. (Take the prescription to the pharmacy.)
steps.hint: 처방전 = prescription.

steps.s: 감기 ___이 뭐예요? 열이 나요. (What are your cold symptoms? I have a fever.)
steps.hint: 증상 = symptoms.

steps.s: 의사 선생님이 ___를 줬어요. (The doctor gave me a shot.)
steps.hint: 주사 = injection/shot.

steps.s: {1}에 가서 {2}를 받았어요. {3}을 가지고 {4}에 갔어요.
steps.hint: I went to the clinic and got a prescription. I took the prescription to the pharmacy.
보험 (保險) = insurance. 건강보험 = health + insurance.

steps.title: From... Until... For... — Time Ranges
steps.desc:
Three particles that define ranges in time and space:

부터: from (starting point)
까지: until/to (ending point)
동안: during/for (duration)

These appear in every Korean schedule, plan, and story.
goals: Combine all three in natural sentences

steps.exampleEn: I work from Monday to Friday.
steps.note:
부터: from (starting point in time or space).

Attaches directly to the noun:
월요일부터: from Monday
아침부터: from morning
9시부터: from 9 o'clock
집에서부터: from home (에서 + 부터 for places)

부터 marks WHERE something STARTS.

까지: until, to, up to.

Attaches directly to the noun:
금요일까지: until Friday
저녁까지: until evening
5시까지: until 5 o'clock
학교까지: to school (distance)

까지 marks WHERE something ENDS.

부터...까지 = from...to/until.

부터...까지: from...to.

9시부터 6시까지: from 9 to 6.
월요일부터 금요일까지: from Monday to Friday.
서울에서부터 부산까지: from Seoul to Busan.

This pair is used constantly:
schedules, travel, stories, descriptions.

동안 (動安/간): during, for (a period of time).

한 시간 동안: for one hour
두 달 동안: for two months
방학 동안: during vacation
여행 동안: during the trip

동안 focuses on HOW LONG, not when.

한 시간 동안 운동했어요.
= I exercised for one hour.

steps.exampleEn: How long does it take to school?
steps.note:
얼마나: how much/long.
걸리다: to take (time).

얼마나 걸려요? = How long does it take?

30분 걸려요: It takes 30 minutes.
한 시간 걸려요: It takes one hour.

학교까지 얼마나 걸려요?
= How long to get to school?
30분 걸려요.
= It takes 30 minutes.

steps.title: 📋 부터/까지/동안 Summary
steps.text:
부터 = FROM (start)
까지 = TO/UNTIL (end)
동안 = FOR/DURING (duration)

TIME:
9시부터 6시까지 (from 9 to 6)
한 시간 동안 (for one hour)

DAYS:
월요일부터 금요일까지 (Mon to Fri)
일주일 동안 (for one week)

PLACES:
서울에서부터 부산까지 (from Seoul to Busan)
학교까지 30분 걸려요 (30 min to school)

부터+까지 = range.
동안 = duration within that range.

steps.hint: 부터 = from. 까지 = to/until.
동안 = for (duration). 두 시간 동안 = for two hours.
걸리다 = to take (time). 30분 걸려요 = takes 30 minutes.

steps.q: 방학 동안 뭐 했어요? 동안 here means:
steps.hint: 동안 = during/for. 방학 동안 = during vacation.
에서 (location) + 부터 (from) = from [place].

steps.s: 9시___6시___일해요. (I work from 9 to 6.)
steps.hint: 부터 = from. (First blank: 9시부터)

steps.s: 한 시간 ___ 운동했어요. (I exercised for one hour.)
steps.hint: 동안 = for (duration).

steps.s: 학교___ 얼마나 걸려요? (How long to school?)
steps.hint: 까지 = to/until (ending point). 학교까지 = to school.

steps.s: 월요일{1} 금요일{2} 일해요. 하루에 8시간 {3} 일해요.
steps.hint: I work from Monday to Friday. I work for 8 hours a day.

opts: Since when have you been in pain?
steps.ans: Since when have you been in pain?
steps.hint: 언제 = when. 부터 = from/since. 언제부터 = since when.
3일 동안 = for 3 days. 드세요 = please take (honorific of 먹다).

steps.title: A New Sound — Softening with -는데
steps.desc:
You're going to start hearing a pattern in Korean:

날씨가 좋은데 같이 나갈까요?
= The weather is nice, so shall we go out together?

아프면 쉬세요 is direct advice.
날씨가 좋은데... softens, suggests, opens a door.

This lesson plants seeds. Don't analyze yet.
Just notice how -는데 makes Korean feel softer and more natural.
goals: Build recognition before formal naming in Unit 13

steps.exampleEn: The weather is nice — shall we take a walk together?
steps.note:
날씨가 좋은데 같이 산책할까요?

좋은데: good + but/so...

-는데/-은데 sets up BACKGROUND before the main point.
It's softer than 그래서 (therefore) or 그런데 (but).

It's like saying: 'Given that the weather is nice...'
The listener knows a suggestion is coming.

⚡ JRTF: -는데 is formally named in Unit 13.
For now: just feel the softening effect.

steps.exampleEn: I have time — shall we watch a movie?
steps.note:
시간이 있는데 영화 볼까요?

있는데: there is + so...

-는데 with action verbs: stem + 는데
있다 → 있는데

-은데 with descriptive verbs (adjectives): stem + (으)ㄴ데
좋다 → 좋은데

Don't memorize the forms yet.
Just notice: -는데 always comes mid-sentence, setting up what comes next.

steps.exampleEn: I'm hungry — what shall we eat?
steps.note:
배가 고픈데 뭐 먹을까요?

고프다 → 고픈데 (ㅡ stem + ㄴ데).

Notice: nobody said 'let's eat.'
-는데 IMPLIES the suggestion without stating it directly.

배가 고파요. 밥 먹어요. = I'm hungry. Let's eat. (direct)
배가 고픈데... = I'm hungry, so... (indirect, softer)

Korean conversation strongly prefers the second form.
This is connected to 눈치 — reading the situation.

steps.title: 🌱 -는데 Preview — What to Notice
steps.text:
Three examples you just saw:

날씨가 좋은데 같이 나갈까요?
시간이 있는데 영화 볼까요?
배가 고픈데 뭐 먹을까요?

Pattern:
[background/situation] + -는데 + [suggestion/question]

-는데 does several things:
1. Sets background: 'given that...'
2. Softens: less direct than 그래서
3. Opens space: invites the listener to respond
4. Shows 눈치: 'I'm hinting, not demanding'

⚡ JRTF: Full explanation and production in Unit 13.
You'll see -는데 many more times before then.
Each time, your feel for it gets stronger.
This is the P26 spiral at work.

deepDive.title: Why -는데 Matters So Much
deepDive.text:
-는데 is arguably the single most important connector in Korean.

It appears in:
• Suggestions: 배가 고픈데 뭐 먹을까요?
• Excuses: 바쁜데 내일 만날까요?
• Contrasts: 한국어는 어려운데 재미있어요.
• Background: 제가 한국에 갔는데 정말 좋았어요.

Native speakers use it in almost every sentence.
Understanding -는데 = understanding Korean conversational flow.

You'll build full command of it in Unit 13.
For now: let it wash over you.

steps.q: 날씨가 좋은데 같이 나갈까요? — -은데 here functions as:
steps.hint: -는데/-은데 sets up background → suggestion follows.

steps.q: 배가 고픈데... — What does this imply?
opts: The speaker is hinting they want to eat
The speaker is asking about food prices
steps.ans: The speaker is hinting they want to eat
steps.hint: -는데 hints and invites response. 'I'm hungry, so...' implies 'let's eat.'

steps.q: This lesson is a ___ for Unit 13:
steps.hint: ⚡ JRTF: Just notice -는데. Full explanation in Unit 13.

steps.s: 날씨가 ___데 같이 산책할까요? (The weather is nice — shall we walk?)
steps.hint: 좋다 (adjective) + (으)ㄴ데 = 좋은데. Don't memorize — just recognize.

steps.s: 시간이 ___데 영화 볼까요? (I have time — shall we watch a movie?)
steps.hint: 있다 (verb) + 는데 = 있는데. Just notice the pattern.

steps.s: {1} 좋은데 같이 {2}? 배가 {3} 뭐 먹을까요?
steps.hint: The weather is nice — shall we go out? I'm hungry — what shall we eat?

steps.q: In Korean conversation, -는데 is preferred over direct statements because:
opts: It shows 눈치 — indirectness and social sensitivity
steps.ans: It shows 눈치 — indirectness and social sensitivity
steps.hint: -는데 softens and hints. Direct = less socially smooth in Korean.

steps.q: Compare: 배가 고파요. 밥 먹어요. vs 배가 고픈데... — which is more natural Korean?
steps.hint: Korean conversation prefers -는데 for its softening, hinting quality.

steps.s: 아프면 쉬세요. 그런데 날씨가 ___데 같이 산책할까요? is softer because of:
steps.hint: 좋다 + (으)ㄴ데 = 좋은데. Background + suggestion.

steps.q: Which sentence uses -는데 to set background?
steps.hint: -는데/-은데 sets background. 좋은데 = nice + so/and...

opts: I'm busy today, so shall we meet tomorrow?
I was busy today and met yesterday
Today is a busy meeting day
steps.ans: I'm busy today, so shall we meet tomorrow?
steps.hint: 바쁘다 + ㄴ데 = 바쁜데. Background → suggestion.

steps.title: 🧠 What to Take Away
steps.text:
You've now seen -는데 three times.

Don't try to produce it yet.
Just listen for it in Korean content.

You'll hear it EVERYWHERE:
• Dramas
• YouTube
• Conversations

Every time you hear -는데, your brain files it.
By Unit 13, naming it will feel like recognition — not new learning.

This is how polyglots learn grammar:
feel it first, name it later.

steps.title: Unit 8 Complete — Health & Grammar Tools
steps.desc:
You've unlocked:

• Body parts and health vocabulary
• 아프다 with correct particle usage (이/가)
• -(으)면 conditional (if/when)
• -(으)ㄹ 수 있다/없다 (can/can't)
• -지 마세요 (don't do)
• 부터/까지/동안 (time ranges)
• 병원 and 약국 vocabulary
• -는데 seeds planted

This quiz tests it all.

steps.hint: ㅡ stem + 면 (ㅡ stays before consonant). 아프면.
Consonant stem + 을 수 있다. 먹을 수 있어요.
Stem + 지 마세요 = please don't. 걱정하지 마세요.
부터 = from. 까지 = to/until.
두 (head) + 통 (pain) = headache.
ㅂ→우: 덥→더우 + 면 = 더우면.
동안 = for/during. 3일 동안 = for 3 days.
처방전 (prescription) → 약국 (pharmacy).
-는데 sets background → softly suggests.

steps.s: ___면 병원에 가세요. (If you're sick, go to the hospital.)
steps.hint: 아프다 → 아프면.

steps.s: 한국어를 ___ 수 있어요. (I can speak Korean.)
steps.hint: 하다 → 할 수 있다.

steps.s: 술 마시___ 마세요. (Don't drink alcohol.)
steps.hint: Stem + 지 마세요 = don't do. 마시 + 지 마세요.

steps.s: 한 시간 ___ 운동했어요. (I exercised for one hour.)
steps.hint: 동안 = for (duration).

steps.s: 감기에 걸렸어요. {1}가 아프고 {2}이 나요. 의사가 말했어요: "약을 3일 {3} 드세요. {4}하지 마세요."
steps.hint: I caught a cold. My throat hurts and I have a fever. The doctor said: 'Take medicine for 3 days. Don't overdo it.'

steps.title: 🎉 Unit 8 Complete
steps.text:
What you can do now:

✅ Describe symptoms and visit a doctor
✅ Use conditionals: 아프면 쉬세요
✅ Express ability: 할 수 있어요 / 없어요
✅ Give negative commands: -지 마세요
✅ Express time ranges: 부터/까지/동안
✅ Navigate Korean healthcare
✅ Recognize -는데 in natural speech

Coming in Unit 9:
미래 시제 (future tense): -(으)ㄹ 거예요
★ Speech levels deep dive
방 culture: 노래방, 피시방, 만화방

화이팅!

steps.q: You've completed Unit 8. Which is the conditional marker?
steps.hint: -(으)면 = if/when. The conditional car on the Train.
